summary: My father, Henry McDiarmid Robinson, was a lawyer from Cumberland County, North Carolina. He practiced law for many years and was the dean of the Cumberland County Bar Association when he died. He was well educated, having attended Bingham School and the University of Virginia, where he was a contemporary of Woodrow Wilson. He was very forward-looking and supportive, especially encouraging women in our family to pursue their ambitions.
cues: dean of Cumberland County Bar Association

summary: Cumberland County, North Carolina, is where my father, Henry McDiarmid Robinson, was a native and practiced law. It was also the place where he served as dean of the Cumberland County Bar Association. Growing up, I was connected to this area through my father's career and family roots.

My mother came from Duplin County, North Carolina. She was Mary Hill Robinson, and her family was well established there. My great aunt Georgie Hicks, who helped raise us after my mother died, was also from Faison in Duplin County and was related to several families in the area.

My father attended Bingham School as a young man before going on to the University of Virginia. It was part of his early education in North Carolina, which was quite good for the times. This foundation helped shape his career as a lawyer and his forward-thinking attitude.

My father studied at the University of Virginia, where he was a contemporary of Woodrow Wilson. He graduated before Wilson was inaugurated president in 1913, and that year we all went to Washington for the inauguration. The University of Virginia was part of my father's distinguished education and influenced our family's outlook.

My mother was educated at Mary Baldwin University in Virginia. After that, she attended Atlantic Female Seminary, earning different certificates. Her education was quite advanced for a woman at that time, reflecting the value our family placed on learning.

My mother attended Atlantic Female Seminary after Mary Baldwin University, where she earned various certificates. This institution was part of her solid educational background, which was notable for women in her era. It helped prepare her for the roles she took on in our family and community.

Faison, North Carolina, is where my great aunt Georgie Hicks was from. She was unmarried and came to live with us to help raise us after my mother died. Faison was also connected to several families, including the Faisons, Hicks, and Hills, who were part of our extended family network.

My great aunt Georgie Hicks attended St. Mary's School in North Carolina. She was there during Sherman's March to the Sea in 1865 and had a memorable experience receiving a message from a Northern soldier. St. Mary's was part of her education and the place where she witnessed important historical events.

During Sherman's March to the Sea in 1865, my great aunt Georgie Hicks was at St. Mary's School. She received a message from a Northern soldier who had come through Faison, where her family lived. This event was frightening for many, as people feared their homes would be burned, but her story shows a personal connection to that historical moment.

After St. Mary's, my great aunt Georgie Hicks went to Charlotte to attend what became Queens University of Charlotte. Her education there was part of the strong tradition of learning in our family. It was remarkable for a woman of her time to have such opportunities.

My older sister graduated from Greensboro Women's College in 1910 with a music degree. She was a top scholar, especially strong in English, and after teaching for a couple of years, she got married and didn't continue working. Greensboro College was an important part of her education and early career.

I taught in Mt. Airy, North Carolina, for one year where I installed the business school and also taught history. The superintendent there, Mr. Turlington, was very kind and supportive. It was part of my early teaching career in the public schools before I decided to pursue law.

After Mt. Airy, I taught history in Salisbury, North Carolina, for a year. The superintendent there, Mr. Allen, had a fine reputation as an educator, and I enjoyed my time teaching in Salisbury. These experiences helped me decide that teaching was not to be my permanent career.

I attended Columbia University for two summers to study law after deciding to pursue a legal career. At Columbia, I had excellent teachers like Mr. Abbott from London who taught real property. There were other women in my classes, including one who was about 60 years old, showing that women were beginning to enter the field despite challenges.

During World War I, I took law courses at night at the Washington College of Law while working in Washington. They had arranged courses for war workers to continue their education, which was very helpful. There was a woman officer in charge, and I was among several women studying law there at the time.

My father had been at the University of Virginia with Woodrow Wilson, who was inaugurated president in 1913. That year, my family went to Washington for Wilson's inauguration, which was a significant event for us. It was also around that time that the women's suffrage movement was gaining momentum, and I encountered many activists during the inauguration.

entity: Women's suffrage in the United States
summary: I witnessed the women's suffrage movement gaining strength around 1913 during Woodrow Wilson's inauguration. I met several women making speeches and advocating for the vote, which inspired me to do more myself. One suffragist I remember meeting was Mary Phillips, who later became president of the National Business and Professional Women's Clubs.

I met Mary Phillips during the women's suffrage movement around 1913. She was one of the women making speeches for suffrage, and I later learned she became president of the National Business and Professional Women's Clubs. Meeting her was part of what encouraged me to pursue my own ambitions.
cues: National Business and Professional Women's Clubs

entity: National Business and Professional Women's Clubs
summary: Mary Phillips, whom I met during the suffrage movement, later became president of the National Business and Professional Women's Clubs. This organization represented professional women striving for recognition and equality, which aligned with my own experiences and aspirations as a woman entering law and professional fields.

entity: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
summary: After being turned down by the University of Virginia because they didn't admit women, I was warmly accepted at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. They adjusted schedules to help me graduate in one year, and I found excellent teachers there. I lived in a rooming house with other women students from places like Dobson, Morganton, and Durham, and we supported each other during that time.

Harvard University did not admit women when I was pursuing my legal education. Along with Yale and Princeton, Harvard was closed to women at that time, which was why I sought admission elsewhere. This reflected the broader challenges women faced entering higher education in law.

Yale University, like Harvard and Princeton, did not admit women when I was studying law. This exclusion was part of the reason I attended other institutions such as Columbia and the University of North Carolina. It was a challenging time for women seeking legal education.

Princeton University was among the schools that did not admit women during my time pursuing legal studies. This limited options for women like me who wanted to enter the legal profession. I had to look to other universities that were more accepting of women students.

While studying at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, I roomed with a woman named Rachel Freeman from Dobson, North Carolina. Rachel was interested in math and planned to be a math teacher. We shared a supportive environment with other women students from various parts of the state.

Elizabeth Taylor from Morganton, North Carolina, was another woman student at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill when I was there. She was interested in dramatics and acting. We lived together in a rooming house and were part of a group of women pursuing diverse careers.

Annie Smith from Durham, North Carolina, was one of the women I lived with at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. She went on to become a very good doctor in Durham. Our group of women students was quite accomplished and supportive of each other.

George Denny was a visitor to the rooming house where I lived at Chapel Hill. He was known for starting the Town Hall in New York and had a reputation for successful talks and broadcasts. His presence added an interesting cultural dimension to our time there.

Town Hall in New York City was started by George Denny, whom I met during my time at Chapel Hill. He organized talks and radio programs that gained quite a reputation. Knowing him gave me a glimpse into the world of public discourse and cultural events beyond the university.

Tom Wolfe occasionally came to the rooming house where I lived at Chapel Hill for meals. It was interesting to meet someone like him during that time, adding to the vibrant atmosphere among students and visitors. These interactions enriched my university experience.

We knew Paul Green during my year at Chapel Hill. He was part of the cultural and academic environment that made that time so interesting. His presence was part of the rich community of scholars and artists around the university.

Judge Walter Clarke wrote me a letter acknowledging that I had led my law class when I took the bar exam. His recognition was a proud moment for me, affirming my efforts as a woman in a male-dominated field. It was more than luck; it reflected the hard work I put into my studies.
